result:
There is a moment, just after the kettle clicks off and just before the first drop of coffee lands in the mug, when the kitchen smells like the inside of an electrical storm. I don’t mean the ozone tang that precedes lightning; I mean something subtler, a sizzling in the nostrils that feels like the whole sky has been folded into a teaspoon of static. That scent is my private equivalent of the Madeleine. It drags me, without courtesy, through wormholes I never asked to map. Suddenly I am seven years old, barefoot on cool linoleum in my grandmother’s house, convinced that the fluorescent light overhead is really a spaceship hatch waiting to beam me up if I stand in exactly the right spot. I spent the entire visit inching my way across the kitchen tiles like a chess piece, trying to triangulate the magical square inch that would open the sky. I never found it, but the quest flavored the air with such voltage that even now, thirty years later, a whiff of overheated kettle resurrects the trembling possibility.

We are, I think, nothing but loose assemblages of such moments, soldered together by happenstance and mislabeled memory. Neuroscience tells me I’m a narrative factory, forever smoothing the jagged edges of perception into a yarn I can carry. But neuroscience doesn’t explain why certain fragments refuse to be planed down. They stay splintered, protruding from the plank of the past like nails ready to snag the bare foot of attention. The kettle smell is one. The creak of a particular staircase at 3 A.M. is another. So is the way the number 47 presents itself whenever I teeter on the lip of decisive change—on hotel doors, receipts, baseball scores, the remaining battery life on my phone the instant I draft a resignation email. I have surreptitiously collected hundreds of photographs of 47s like an entomologist pinning rare beetles. The rational part of my mind rolls its eyes, calls it apophenia, a stroboscopic glitch in pattern recognition software. The rest of me keeps snapping pictures, because the alternative—that the universe is not speaking but stuttering—feels lonelier.

Loneliness, incidentally, is another word I keep misplacing. The dictionary insists it signifies isolation, yet the loneliest I have ever felt was in a packed auditorium, shoulder-to-sweaty-shoulder with 800 fellow human beings who cheered on a keynote speaker I found vacuous. The speaker cracked a joke about machine learning that was neither about learning nor machines, and the room erupted. In that instant I sensed a continental drift between my skull and the next one, an imperceptible migration that left me speaking a dead dialect. I wanted to stand and shout, “This isn’t funny! Can’t you see the typo in the slide?” Instead I scrolled idly through my camera roll, landed on yesterday’s 47, and felt absurdly comforted. The number glowed on the screen like a night-light for cosmic orphans.

I’ve come to suspect that everyone keeps a private numinous junk drawer. One colleague confessed to hoarding ticket stubs because the barcodes feel like Braille messages from parallel lives. Another quietly photographs the backs of strangers’ heads, convinced that the rear view of a person reveals more honesty than the face. My mother saves the silica-gel packets that arrive in shoeboxes, miniature translucent pillows labeled DO NOT EAT, and scatters them among photo albums as though to ward off the mildew of grief. We curate these irrational archives because to throw them away would be to acknowledge that the universe has no secret handshake, that we are inducted into no hidden fraternity, that the only password is the beat of blood in the ears and the only membership card is breath.

Still, I catch myself attempting to craft a skeleton key. Suppose I thread all my 47s onto a necklace; would it become a rosary capable of unlocking statistical coincidence? Suppose I learn the exact decibel level of that 3 A.M. staircase creak; could I replicate it at will and summon the hush of possibility whenever life feels too garish? The project is hopeless, of course, but hopelessness has never been a successful deterrent. My pockets continue to fill with pebbles shaped like hearts, with receipts that sum to palindromes, with subway tokens from cities whose names I can’t pronounce. Each object feels like a breadcrumb, and I a bewildered Hansel, convinced that if only I collect enough of them I will someday look down and see the whole trail spelling out a sentence: “You were here for a reason.”

Technology, supposedly the antidote to superstition, merely offers new arenas for it. My phone’s algorithm notices the 47 obsession and, with the earnest helpfulness of a golden retriever, keeps serving articles about the 47th president, the 47th chromosome, a restaurant on 47th street. The machine thinks it is mirroring me, but mirrors have no sense of humor; they simply bounce the ray back. The algorithm does not know that the number’s power lies in its unsummonable randomness, the way it ambushes rather than answers. Predictability kills the miracle; the map murders the territory. I click “not interested,” but the gesture feels like swatting at a ghost with a tennis racket.

Sometimes, late at night, I zoom far out on Google Earth until the roofs of my neighborhood compress into pixelated confetti. From that height it is easy to imagine myself as a single punctuation mark in a sentence spoken by something incomprehensibly large. The thought ought to be terrifying, yet it soothes me in the way that lying on the seashore and watching the stars feels soothing: the scale annihilates the to-do list. Nothing I buy, break, or become will show up from that orbital remove. The mortgage, the unread emails, the voicemail I’ve been avoiding—all dissolve into the iridescent blue-green swirl. From space, the difference between a shopping mall and a cathedral is smaller than a pixel. The knowledge feels illicit, like a cheat code whispered in a childhood video game: Up, Up, Down, Down, Left, Right, Left, Right, Breathe.

And yet the pendulum always swings back. The earth re-zooms, the kettle re-boils, the 47 re-appears. I wake up to the smell of Monday commute and remember that I owe someone money, that a tooth in the back of my mouth sings whenever I drink ice water, that the novel I promised myself I’d finish by thirty-five remains a folder of orphaned paragraphs. Gravity, the original narcissist, insists on pronouncing its name in every dropped spoon, every sagging jowl, every digital calendar ping announcing another Tuesday that tastes suspiciously like last Tuesday. I read somewhere that time is not a river but a frozen lake, and the sensation of motion is merely our consciousness sliding across the surface. If that is true, then déjà vu is the moment we glimpse the edge of a skate mark we carved on an earlier pass.

Occasionally I meet someone who claims never to experience déjà vu, never to count ceiling tiles, never to chase the uncanny. These people fascinate and terrify me in equal measure. They remind me of those who can stare directly at the sun without tearing up, or who eat grapefruit without sugar, or who board airplanes without whispering the statistical mantra I am more likely to be struck by lightning. They stride through life armed with the same nonchalance with which they button shirts. I nod politely while secretly wondering if they are the NPCs, or if I am, or whether the universe simply requires a certain ratio of mystics to accountants the way a forest requires both mycelium and owls.

Either way, tomorrow the kettle will click again. The staircase will creak. The number will flicker somewhere on the periphery of sight. And I, conspirator with the inanimate, will pocket the new scrap and carry it like contraband toward the next unsuspecting minute, confident only of this: the story is incomplete, the breadcrumbs are edible, and somewhere in the circuitry of an ordinary Tuesday the spaceship hatch is still waiting, impatient for us to find the precise square inch where the linoleum yields to sky.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want for 1000 words.
